content:
# Genesis Chapter 31 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 17v–18v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 27–29 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0031 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0032 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0033

---

*Laban's Sons — Changed Attitude — The LORD Commands Jacob to Return (vv. 1–3)*

**31:1** And he heard [וַיִּשְׁמַע] the words of [אֶת-דִּבְרֵי] Laban's sons [בְּנֵי-לָבָן] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Jacob has taken [לָקַח יַעֲקֹב] all that [אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר] was our father's [לְאָבִינוּ] — and from what was our father's [וּמֵאֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ] he has made [עָשָׂה] all this [אֵת כָּל-הַכָּבֹד] glory [הַכָּבֹד] this.

**31:2** And Jacob saw [וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב] the face of [אֶת-פְּנֵי] Laban [לָבָן] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] it was not toward him [אֵינֶנּוּ עִמּוֹ] as formerly [כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם].

**31:3** And the LORD said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב]: Return [שׁוּב] to the land of [אֶל-אֶרֶץ] your fathers [אֲבוֹתֶיךָ] and to your kindred [וּלְמוֹלַדְתֶּךָ] — and I will be with you [וְאֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ].

---

*Jacob Calls Rachel and Leah — His Speech Recounting Laban's Treatment (vv. 4–16)*

**31:4** And Jacob sent [וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב] and called [וַיִּקְרָא] to Rachel [לְרָחֵל] and to Leah [וּלְלֵאָה] — to the field [הַשָּׂדֶה] to his flock [אֶל-צֹאנוֹ].

**31:5** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to them [לָהֶן]: I see [רֹאֶה אָנֹכִי] the face of [אֶת-פְּנֵי] your father [אֲבִיכֶן] — that it is not toward me [כִּי-אֵינֶנּוּ אֵלַי] as formerly [כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם] — and [וְ] the God of [אֱלֹהֵי] my father [אָבִי] has been with me [הָיָה עִמָּדִי].

**31:6** And you yourselves know [וְאַתֵּן יְדַעְתֶּן] that with all my strength [כִּי בְכָל-כֹּחִי] I have served [עָבַדְתִּי] your father [אֶת-אֲבִיכֶן].

**31:7** But your father [וַאֲבִיכֶן] has deceived me [הֵתֶל בִּי] and changed [וְהֶחֱלִף] my wages [אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי] ten times [עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים] — yet [וְ] God has not allowed him [לֹא-נְתָנוֹ אֱלֹהִים] to harm me [לְהָרַע עִמָּדִי].

**31:8** If thus [אִם-כֹּה] he said [יֹאמַר]: The speckled ones [נְקֻדִּים] shall be your wages [יִהְיֶה שְׂכָרֶךָ] — then all the flock [וְיָלְדוּ כָל-הַצֹּאן] bore speckled ones [נְקֻדִּים] — and if [וְאִם-כֹּה] thus [כֹּה] he said [יֹאמַר]: The striped ones [עֲקֻדִּים] shall be your wages [יִהְיֶה שְׂכָרֶךָ] — then all the flock [וְיָלְדוּ כָל-הַצֹּאן] bore striped ones [עֲקֻדִּים].

**31:9** And God has taken away [וַיַּצֵּל אֱלֹהִים] the livestock of [אֶת-מִקְנֵה] your father [אֲבִיכֶן] and given [וַיִּתֶּן] [them] to me [לִי].

**31:10** And it was [וַיְהִי] at the time [בְּעֵת] the flock was in heat [יַחֵם הַצֹּאן] — and I lifted up [וָאֶשָּׂא] my eyes [עֵינַי] and saw [וָאֶרֶא] in the dream [בַּחֲלוֹם] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — the male goats [הָעַתֻּדִים] that were mounting the flock [הָעֹלִים עַל-הַצֹּאן] — striped [עֲקֻדִּים] speckled [נְקֻדִּים] and dappled [וּבְרֻדִּים].

**31:11** And the angel of God [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלַי מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים] said to me [וַיֹּאמֶר] in the dream [בַּחֲלוֹם]: Jacob! [יַעֲקֹב] And I said [וָאֹמַר]: Here I am [הִנֵּנִי].

**31:12** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Lift up now [שָׂא-נָא] your eyes [עֵינֶיךָ] and see [וּרְאֵה] — all the male goats [כָּל-הָעַתֻּדִים] that are mounting the flock [הָעֹלִים עַל-הַצֹּאן] — striped [עֲקֻדִּים] speckled [נְקֻדִּים] and dappled [וּבְרֻדִּים] — for [כִּי] I have seen [רָאִיתִי] all that [אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר] Laban [לָבָן] is doing [עֹשֶׂה] to you [לָּךְ].

**31:13** I am the God of Bethel [אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית-אֵל] — where you anointed [אֲשֶׁר מָשַׁחְתָּ] a pillar [שָׁם מַצֵּבָה] — where you vowed [אֲשֶׁר נָדַרְתָּ לִּי] to me there [שָׁם] a vow [נֶדֶר] — now rise [קוּם עַתָּה] go out [צֵא] from this land [מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] and return [וְשׁוּב] to the land of [אֶל-אֶרֶץ] your kindred [מוֹלַדְתֶּךָ].

**31:14** And Rachel answered [וַתַּעַן רָחֵל] and Leah [וְלֵאָה] and said [וַתֹּאמַרְנָה] to him [לוֹ]: Is there yet [הַעוֹד לָנוּ] for us [לָנוּ] a portion [חֵלֶק] or [אוֹ] inheritance [נַחֲלָה] in the house of [בְּבֵית] our father [אָבִינוּ]?

**31:15** Are we not [הֲלוֹא] reckoned [נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ] as foreigners [לוֹ נָכְרִיּוֹת] to him [לוֹ] — for [כִּי] he has sold us [מְכָרָנוּ] and has also [וַיֹּאכַל גַּם] eaten up [אָכוֹל] our silver [אֶת-כַּסְפֵּנוּ]?

**31:16** For [כִּי] all the wealth [כָּל-הָעֹשֶׁר] that God has taken away [אֲשֶׁר הִצִּיל אֱלֹהִים] from our father [מֵאָבִינוּ] — it is ours [לָנוּ הוּא] and our children's [וּלְבָנֵינוּ] — and now [וְעַתָּה] all that [כֹּל אֲשֶׁר] God has said [אָמַר אֱלֹהִים] to you [אֵלֶיךָ] — do [עֲשֵׂה].

---

*The Flight — Rachel Steals the Teraphim (vv. 17–21)*

**31:17** And Jacob rose [וַיָּקָם יַעֲקֹב] and set [וַיִּשָּׂא] his sons [אֶת-בָּנָיו] and his wives [וְאֶת-נָשָׁיו] upon the camels [עַל-הַגְּמַלִּים].

**31:18** And he drove away [וַיִּנְהַג] all his livestock [אֶת-כָּל-מִקְנֵהוּ] and all his substance [וְאֶת-כָּל-רְכֻשׁוֹ] that he had gained [אֲשֶׁר רָכָשׁ] — the livestock of [מִקְנֵה] his acquisition [קִנְיָנוֹ] that he had gotten [אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ] in Paddan-aram [בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם] — to go [לָבוֹא] to Isaac [אֶל-יִצְחָק] his father [אָבִיו] — to the land of [אֶרֶץ] Canaan [כְּנָעַן].

**31:19** Now Laban had gone [וְלָבָן הָלַךְ] to shear [לִגְזֹז] his sheep [אֶת-צֹאנוֹ] — and Rachel stole [וַתִּגְנֹב רָחֵל] the teraphim [אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים] that were [אֲשֶׁר] her father's [לְאָבִיהָ].

**31:20** And Jacob stole the heart of [וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת-לֵב] Laban the Aramean [לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי] — in that [עַל] he did not tell him [בְּלִי הִגִּיד לוֹ] that he was fleeing [כִּי בֹרֵחַ הוּא].

**31:21** And he fled [וַיִּבְרַח הוּא] — he and all that was his [וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] — and he rose [וַיָּקָם] and crossed [וַיַּעֲבֹר] the River [אֶת-הַנָּהָר] — and set [וַיָּשֶׂם] his face [אֶת-פָּנָיו] toward the hill country of [הַר] Gilead [הַגִּלְעָד].

---

*Laban Pursues — God Warns Him in a Dream (vv. 22–25)*

**31:22** And it was told [וַיֻּגַּד] to Laban [לְלָבָן] on the third day [בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי] that Jacob had fled [כִּי בָרַח יַעֲקֹב].

**31:23** And he took [וַיִּקַּח] his kinsmen with him [אֶת-אֶחָיו עִמּוֹ] and pursued him [וַיִּרְדֹּף אַחֲרָיו] a journey of seven days [דֶּרֶךְ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים] and caught up with him [וַיַּדְבֵּק אֹתוֹ] in the hill country of [בְּהַר] Gilead [הַגִּלְעָד].

**31:24** And God came [וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים] to Laban the Aramean [אֶל-לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי] in a dream of the night [בַּחֲלֹם הַלָּיְלָה] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to him [לוֹ]: Guard yourself [הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ] lest you speak [פֶּן-תְּדַבֵּר] to Jacob [עִם-יַעֲקֹב] either good [מִטּוֹב] or evil [עַד-רָע].

**31:25** And Laban overtook [וַיַּשֵּׂג לָבָן] Jacob [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] — now Jacob had pitched [וְיַעֲקֹב תָּקַע] his tent [אֶת-אָהֳלוֹ] in the hill country [בָּהָר] — and Laban with his kinsmen [וְלָבָן תָּקַע] had pitched [תָּקַע] in the hill country of [בְּהַר] Gilead [הַגִּלְעָד].

---

*The Confrontation — Laban's Accusation and Jacob's Defense (vv. 26–42)*

**31:26** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב]: What have you done [מֶה עָשִׂיתָ] — that you stole my heart [וַתִּגְנֹב אֶת-לְבָבִי] and drove away [וַתְּנַהֵג] my daughters [אֶת-בְּנֹתַי] like captives of the sword [כִּשְׁבֻיוֹת חָרֶב]?

**31:27** Why did you flee [לָמָּה נַחְבֵּאתָ לָבְרֹחַ] secretly [נַחְבֵּאתָ] and steal away [וַתִּגְנֹב אֹתִי] from me [אֹתִי] — and did not tell me [וְלֹא-הִגַּדְתָּ לִּי] — that I might have sent you away [כִּי הָיִיתִי מְשַׁלֵּחֲךָ] with rejoicing [בְּשִׂמְחָה] and with songs [וּבְשִׁרִים] — with tambourine [בְּתֹף] and with lyre [וּבְכִנּוֹר]?

**31:28** And you did not allow me [וְלֹא נְטַשְׁתַּנִי] to kiss [לְנַשֵּׁק] my sons [לְבָנַי] and my daughters [וְלִבְנֹתָי] — now you have acted foolishly [עַתָּה הִסְכַּלְתָּ עֲשׂוֹ].

**31:29** It is in the power of [יֶשׁ-לְאֵל יָדִי] my hand [יָדִי] to do you harm [לַעֲשׂוֹת עִמָּכֶם רָע] — but the God of [וֵאלֹהֵי] your father [אֲבִיכֶם] said [אָמַר] to me last night [אֶמֶשׁ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Guard yourself [הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ] from speaking [מִדַּבֵּר] to Jacob [עִם-יַעֲקֹב] either good [מִטּוֹב] or evil [עַד-רָע].

**31:30** And now [וְעַתָּה] — going you have gone [הָלֹךְ הָלַכְתָּ] — for [כִּי] you longed greatly [נִכְסֹף נִכְסַפְתָּה] for the house of [לְבֵית] your father [אָבִיךָ] — but why [לָמָּה] did you steal [גָנַבְתָּ] my gods [אֶת-אֱלֹהָי]?

**31:31** And Jacob answered [וַיַּעַן יַעֲקֹב] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to Laban [לְלָבָן]: For [כִּי] I was afraid [יָרֵאתִי] — for [כִּי] I said [אָמַרְתִּי]: Lest [פֶּן] you take [תִּגְזֹל] your daughters [אֶת-בְּנֹתֶיךָ] from me [מֵעִמִּי].

**31:32** With whomever [עִם אֲשֶׁר] you find [תִּמְצָא] your gods [אֶת-אֱלֹהֶיךָ] — he shall not live [לֹא יִחְיֶה] — before our kinsmen [נֶגֶד אַחֵינוּ] discern [הַכֶּר-לְךָ] what is yours [מָה עִמָּדִי] and take it [וְקַח-לָךְ] — for Jacob [וְלֹא-יָדַע יַעֲקֹב] did not know [כִּי] that Rachel had stolen them [רָחֵל גְּנָבָתַם].

**31:33** And Laban went in [וַיָּבֹא לָבָן] to Jacob's tent [בְּאֹהֶל יַעֲקֹב] and into the tent of [וּבְאֹהֶל] Leah [לֵאָה] and into the tent of [וּבְאֹהֶל] the two maidservants [שְׁתֵּי הָאֲמָהֹת] — and he did not find [וְלֹא מָצָא] — and he went out [וַיֵּצֵא] from Leah's tent [מֵאֹהֶל לֵאָה] and entered [וַיָּבֹא] Rachel's tent [בְּאֹהֶל רָחֵל].

**31:34** Now Rachel had taken [וְרָחֵל לָקְחָה] the teraphim [אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים] and put them [וַתְּשִׂמֵם] in the saddle-bag of the camel [בְּכַר הַגָּמָל] and sat upon them [וַתֵּשֶׁב עֲלֵיהֶם] — and Laban felt through [וַיְמַשֵּׁשׁ לָבָן] all the tent [אֶת-כָּל-הָאֹהֶל] and did not find [וְלֹא מָצָא].

**31:35** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר] to her father [אֶל-אָבִיהָ]: Let it not be displeasing [אַל-יִחַר] in the eyes of [בְּעֵינֵי] my lord [אֲדֹנִי] that I am not able [כִּי לֹא-אוּכַל] to rise [קוּם] before you [מִפָּנֶיךָ] — for the way of women [כִּי-דֶרֶךְ נָשִׁים] is upon me [לִי] — and he searched [וַיְחַפֵּשׂ] and did not find [וְלֹא מָצָא] the teraphim [אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים].

**31:36** And Jacob was angry [וַיִּחַר לְיַעֲקֹב] and contended with [וַיָּרֶב] Laban [בְּלָבָן] — and Jacob answered [וַיַּעַן יַעֲקֹב] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to Laban [לְלָבָן]: What is my transgression [מַה-פִּשְׁעִי] — what is my sin [מַה חַטָּאתִי] — that you have pursued hot [כִּי דָלַקְתָּ] after me [אַחֲרָי]?

**31:37** For you have felt through [כִּי-מִשַּׁשְׁתָּ] all my goods [אֶת-כָּל-כֵּלַי] — what have you found [מַה-מָּצָאתָ] of all the goods of [מִכֹּל כְּלֵי] your house [בֵּיתֶךָ]? Set [שִׂים] it here [כֹה] before my kinsmen [נֶגֶד אַחַי] and your kinsmen [וְנֶגֶד אַחֶיךָ] and let them decide [וְיוֹכִיחוּ] between the two of us [בֵּין שְׁנֵינוּ].

**31:38** These twenty years [זֶה עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה] I have been with you [אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ] — your ewes [רְחֵלֶיךָ] and your female goats [וְעִזֶּיךָ] have not miscarried [לֹא שִׁכֵּלוּ] — and the rams of [וְאֵילֵי] your flock [צֹאנְךָ] I have not eaten [לֹא אָכָלְתִּי].

**31:39** That which was torn [טְרֵפָה] I did not bring [לֹא-הֵבֵאתִי] to you [אֵלֶיךָ] — I bore [אָנֹכִי אֲחַטֶּנָּה] the loss of it [אֲחַטֶּנָּה] — from my hand [מִיָּדִי] you required it [תְּבַקְשֶׁנָּה] — whether stolen by day [גְּנֻבְתִי יוֹם] or stolen by night [וּגְנֻבְתִי לָיְלָה].

**31:40** I was [הָיִיתִי] — by day [בַּיּוֹם] the heat [חֹרֶב] consumed me [אֲכָלַנִי] and the frost [וְקֶרַח] by night [בַּלָּיְלָה] — and my sleep [וַתִּדַּד שְׁנָתִי] fled [וַתִּדַּד] from my eyes [מֵעֵינָי].

**31:41** This [זֶה] to me [לִי] twenty years [עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה] in your house [בְּבֵיתֶךָ] — I served you [עֲבַדְתִּיךָ] fourteen years [אַרְבַּע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה] for your two daughters [בִּשְׁתֵּי בְנֹתֶיךָ] and six years [וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים] for your flock [בְּצֹאנֶךָ] — and you changed [וַתַּחֲלֵף] my wages [אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי] ten times [עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים].

**31:42** Were it not [לוּלֵי] the God of [אֱלֹהֵי] my father [אָבִי] — the God of Abraham [אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם] and the Fear of Isaac [וּפַחַד יִצְחָק] — had been for me [הָיָה לִי] — for [כִּי] now [עַתָּה] empty-handed [רֵיקָם] you would have sent me away [שִׁלַּחְתָּנִי] — God has seen [רָאָה אֱלֹהִים] my affliction [אֶת-עָנְיִי] and the labor of [וְאֶת-יְגִיעַ] my hands [כַּפַּי] — and he reproved [וַיּוֹכַח] you last night [אֶמֶשׁ].

---

*The Covenant at the Heap — Jegar-sahadutha and Gal-ed — Mizpah (vv. 43–55)*

**31:43** And Laban answered [וַיַּעַן לָבָן] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב]: The daughters [הַבָּנוֹת] are my daughters [בְּנֹתַי] and the sons [וְהַבָּנִים] are my sons [בָנַי] and the flocks [וְהַצֹּאן] are my flocks [צֹאנִי] and all that you see [וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה רֹאֶה] is mine [לִי-הוּא] — but to my daughters [וְלִבְנֹתַי] — what can I do [מָה-אֶעֱשֶׂה] to these today [לָאֵלֶּה הַיּוֹם] or to their sons [אוֹ לִבְנֵיהֶן] that they have fathered [אֲשֶׁר יָלָדוּ]?

**31:44** And now [וְעַתָּה] come [לְכָה] — let us make [נִכְרְתָה] a covenant [בְּרִית] — I and you [אֲנִי וָאָתָּה] — and let it be [וְהָיָה] a witness [לְעֵד] between me and you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ].

**31:45** And Jacob took [וַיִּקַּח יַעֲקֹב] a stone [אֶבֶן] and set it up [וַיְרִימֶהָ] as a pillar [מַצֵּבָה].

**31:46** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to his kinsmen [לְאֶחָיו]: Gather [לִקְטוּ] stones [אֲבָנִים] — and they took [וַיִּקְחוּ] stones [אֲבָנִים] and made [וַיַּעֲשׂוּ] a heap [גָּל] — and they ate [וַיֹּאכְלוּ] there [שָׁם] upon the heap [עַל-הַגָּל].

**31:47** And Laban called it [וַיִּקְרָא-לוֹ לָבָן] Jegar-sahadutha [יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא] — and Jacob called it [וְיַעֲקֹב קָרָא לוֹ] Gal-ed [גַּלְעֵד].

**31:48** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן]: This heap [הַגַּל הַזֶּה] is witness [עֵד] between me and you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ] today [הַיּוֹם] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] he called [קָרָא] its name [שְׁמוֹ] Gal-ed [גַּלְעֵד].

**31:49** And the Mizpah [וְהַמִּצְפָּה] — for he said [אֲשֶׁר אָמַר]: May the LORD watch [יִצֶף יְהוָה] between me and you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ] when we are hidden [כִּי נִסָּתֵר] one from the other [אִישׁ מֵרֵעֵהוּ].

**31:50** If you afflict [אִם-תְּעַנֶּה] my daughters [אֶת-בְּנֹתַי] or if you take [וְאִם-תִּקַּח] wives [נָשִׁים] in addition to [עַל] my daughters [בְּנֹתַי] — no man [אֵין אִישׁ] is with us [עִמָּנוּ] — see [רְאֵה] — God is witness [אֱלֹהִים עֵד] between me and you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ].

**31:51** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב]: Behold [הִנֵּה] this heap [הַגַּל הַזֶּה] and behold [וְהִנֵּה] the pillar [הַמַּצֵּבָה] that I have set [אֲשֶׁר יָרִיתִי] between me [בֵּינִי] and you [וּבֵינֶךָ].

**31:52** Witness is this heap [עֵד הַגַּל הַזֶּה] and witness is the pillar [וְעֵדָה הַמַּצֵּבָה] — that I will not pass over [אִם-אֲנִי לֹא-אֶעֱבֹר] to you [אֵלֶיךָ] this heap [אֶת-הַגַּל הַזֶּה] and that you will not pass over [וְאִם-אַתָּה לֹא-תַעֲבֹר] to me [אֵלַי] this heap [אֶת-הַגַּל הַזֶּה] and this pillar [וְאֶת-הַמַּצֵּבָה הַזֹּאת] for evil [לְרָעָה].

**31:53** The God of Abraham [אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם] and the God of Nahor [וֵאלֹהֵי נָחוֹר] — the God of their father [אֱלֹהֵי אֲבִיהֶם] — shall judge [יִשְׁפְּטוּ] between us [בֵּינֵינוּ] — and Jacob swore [וַיִּשָּׁבַע יַעֲקֹב] by the Fear of [בְּפַחַד] his father Isaac [אָבִיו יִצְחָק].

**31:54** And Jacob offered [וַיִּזְבַּח יַעֲקֹב] a sacrifice [זֶבַח] on the mountain [בָּהָר] and called [וַיִּקְרָא] his kinsmen [לְאֶחָיו] to eat bread [לֶאֱכָל-לָחֶם] — and they ate bread [וַיֹּאכְלוּ לֶחֶם] and stayed the night [וַיָּלִינוּ] on the mountain [בָּהָר].

**31:55** And Laban rose early [וַיַּשְׁכֵּם לָבָן] in the morning [בַּבֹּקֶר] and kissed [וַיְנַשֵּׁק] his sons [לְבָנָיו] and his daughters [וְלִבְנֹתָיו] and blessed them [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֶתְהֶם] — and Laban went [וַיֵּלֶךְ לָבָן] and returned [וַיָּשָׁב] to his place [לִמְקֹמוֹ].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 17v–18v:**

LC page 1 (folio 17v, GEN_031_page_001.jpg) is photographed inverted (rotated 180°), consistent with the West Semitic Research Project's archiving pattern for this folio series. Once re-oriented, the page carries the close of Genesis 30 in the upper portion and the opening of Genesis 31 through approximately verse 19, covering Laban's sons' complaint, Jacob's changed perception of Laban, the LORD's command to return, Jacob's speech to Rachel and Leah, the flight, and Rachel's theft of the teraphim. The three-column Sephardic square script is fully vocalized and cantillated throughout. The name יְהוָה (YHWH) is clearly legible with its traditional Qere vocalization in v. 3 (the LORD's command: שׁוּב). The word תְּרָפִים (teraphim) is visible in v. 19.

LC page 2 (folio 18r, GEN_031_page_002.jpg) is in standard orientation. It carries Genesis 31:20–42 approximately, covering Jacob's theft of Laban's heart, the flight across the Euphrates toward Gilead, Laban's pursuit, the divine warning in the dream (v. 24: מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע), the confrontation dialogue, Rachel's concealment of the teraphim beneath the camel-saddle, and Jacob's climactic twenty-year speech culminating in the Pachad Yitschaq epithet in v. 42. The text is densely packed across the three columns; masoretic parasha markers are visible in the margins. The unusual divine epithet פַחַד יִצְחָק (Pachad Yitschaq) is legible in v. 42.

LC page 3 (folio 18v, GEN_031_page_003.jpg) is photographed inverted (rotated 180°). Once re-oriented, it carries the covenant conclusion of Genesis 31 (vv. 43–55) and the opening of Genesis 32. The dual place-name verse — v. 47, with both the Aramaic יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא and the Hebrew גַּלְעֵד — is visible, as are the Mizpah covenant terms and the closing scene of Laban's departure. The chapter ends without a parashah break marker before Genesis 32 opens.

---

**31:1 — "All this glory" (*'et kol-hakkavod hazzeh*, אֵת כָּל-הַכָּבֹד הַזֶּה):**
The word כָּבוֹד (*kavod*) means "glory, weight, honor, wealth, substance." Here it denotes Jacob's accumulated material wealth — what the sons of Laban are bitterly calling Jacob's "honor" or "glory" is precisely what their father lost. The idiom is emphatic: Jacob has made for himself *all this glory/wealth* from what belonged to *our father*. The LXX Vaticanus reads *tēn doxan tautēn* (τὴν δόξαν ταύτην) — "this glory" — preserving the same word *doxa*, which in Greek also carries both the sense of material wealth/renown and divine radiance. EMET renders *kavod* as "glory" to keep the full weight of the word: it is simultaneously material wealth, honor, and splendor. The word is chosen by the narrator (or the sons in their bitterness) with rhetorical punch.

---

**31:2 — "As formerly" (*kitĕmol shilshom*, כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם):**
The Hebrew idiom כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם ("as yesterday, as the day before yesterday") is the standard biblical expression for "as before, as in former times" — cf. Genesis 31:5 (the same phrase appears again immediately), Exodus 5:7–8, 14, 1 Samuel 14:21. It is a frozen idiomatic compound meaning "as [it was] formerly / previously." EMET renders it "as formerly" throughout rather than the more cumbersome literal "as yesterday, as the day before yesterday." The LXX Vaticanus reads *chthes kai tritēn hēmeran* (χθὲς καὶ τρίτην ἡμέραν) — "yesterday and the day before yesterday" — preserving the literal idiom. The repetition of the phrase in both v. 2 (Jacob observing Laban) and v. 5 (Jacob reporting to his wives) is deliberate narrative emphasis: Laban's attitude has changed, and Jacob has noticed.

---

**31:7 — "Ten times" (*'aseret monim*, עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים):**
The phrase עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (*'aseret monim*) is literally "ten-of-times" — using *monim* (מֹנִים), the plural of *māneh* or *mon* (a count/number). This is the idiom for "ten times" or "many times" — a round number conveying frequent repetition. Jacob repeats the same accusation in v. 41: "you changed my wages ten times." Whether Jacob means a literal ten distinct wage-changes or uses "ten" as a rhetorical superlative for "many times over" is debated; the text presents it as a serious accusation of systematic exploitation. The LXX Vaticanus reads *deka amnas* (δέκα ἄμνας) — "ten lambs" — a striking reading that does not match the MT and may reflect a different *Vorlage* or a translator's interpretation of *monim* as a weight/measure of lambs. The MT *monim* (times/counts) is the more straightforward reading and is supported by the internal consistency of the narrative. EMET follows the MT.

---

**31:10–12 — The Dream of the Striped Male Goats — Retrospective Revelation:**
The dream sequence Jacob reports to Rachel and Leah provides the theological resolution to the breeding episode of Genesis 30:37–42. Jacob reveals that God showed him in a dream during the mating season that the male goats mounting the flock were *striped, speckled, and dappled* — that is, God was directing the genetics of the flock supernaturally. The peeled-rod technique of chapter 30 was Jacob's action, but the supernatural blessing came from God's direct intervention demonstrated in the dream. The word for the male goats — עַתֻּדִים (*'attudim*, mature he-goats) — is different from the word used in 30:35 for the males Laban separated out (תְּיָשִׁים *tĕyāshim*, male goats/bucks). A new term, *'attudim*, appears here in the dream as the dominant breeders. The third color category — בְּרֻדִּים (*bĕrudim*, "dappled, spotted like hail") — appears in the dream but was not part of Jacob's original agreement (30:32 mentioned only speckled, spotted, and dark). This additional category in the dream suggests God's bounty exceeded even what Jacob had bargained for.

---

**31:13 — "I am the God of Bethel" (*'anokhi ha-El Bet-El*, אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית-אֵל):**
The divine self-identification is remarkable: not "I am YHWH" nor "I am Elohim" but "I am *ha-El Bet-El*" — "the God of Bethel" — the God of *the place*, referencing the specific theophany at Bethel in Genesis 28:10–22 where Jacob anointed a pillar and made a vow. The divine name here is הָאֵל (*ha-El*) — "the God," using the ancient singular *El* (אֵל) rather than the plural *Elohim* — a marker of the oldest divine naming tradition. The angel's speech conflates what in Genesis 28 were Jacob's own vow and God's own promise: "where you anointed a pillar — where you vowed to me a vow." This collapse of the two parties' actions into a single remembered location is theologically significant: the vow and the anointing are cited as the basis of Jacob's obligation to return. The LXX Vaticanus reads *egō eimi ho Theos ho ophtheis soi en topō Theou* (ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ Θεὸς ὁ ὀφθείς σοι ἐν τόπῳ Θεοῦ) — "I am the God who appeared to you in the place of God" — a paraphrase that renders "Bethel" (Hebrew: "house of God") as *en topō Theou* ("in the place of God"), circumventing the place-name.

---

**31:19 — Teraphim (*hattĕraphim*, הַתְּרָפִים) — Household Gods:**
The תְּרָפִים (*tĕraphim*) are household cult objects — small figurines of ancestor-gods or household deities, used for divination and as symbols of family continuity and inheritance. They appear elsewhere in the OT: Judges 17–18 (Micah's household shrine), 1 Samuel 19:13 (Michal puts teraphim in David's bed), Hosea 3:4 (Israel without teraphim), Zechariah 10:2 (teraphim speak emptily). Their legal significance in the ancient Near East has been debated: Nuzi texts show that possession of household gods could confer inheritance rights, which may explain why Rachel steals them — to secure Jacob's claim to a portion of Laban's estate. However, the text does not explicitly invoke this legal background; it may simply portray Rachel as reluctant to leave her father's gods behind, or as preventing Laban from using them to track Jacob by divination (consistent with Laban's character as a diviner, cf. 30:27). EMET transliterates *tĕraphim* as "teraphim" throughout, per the project protocol for terms with no English equivalent, rather than rendering them "household gods" or "idols." The LXX Vaticanus reads *ta eidōla* (τὰ εἴδωλα) — "the idols/images" — a more polemical rendering that reflects the LXX translators' theological perspective. The MT uses the neutral technical term *tĕraphim* without editorial comment.

---

**31:20 — "Jacob stole the heart of Laban" (*wayyignov Ya'akov 'et-lev Lavan*, וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת-לֵב לָבָן):**
The verb גָּנַב (*gānav*, "to steal") is used three times in rapid succession in this passage in close literary proximity: Rachel "stole" (*wayyignov*) the teraphim (v. 19); Jacob "stole the heart of" (*wayyignov... 'et-lev*) Laban (v. 20); and Laban later accuses Jacob "you stole my heart" (*vayyignov 'et-lĕvavî*, v. 26). The phrase "to steal the heart" (גָּנַב אֶת-לֵב) is a Hebrew idiom for deception by concealment — not to manipulate someone's emotions but to deceive them by acting without their knowledge. The idiom is also used in 2 Samuel 15:6 for Absalom who "stole the hearts of the men of Israel" by winning them over. Here it means Jacob deceived Laban by fleeing without telling him. EMET renders this literally as "stole the heart of" to preserve the idiom in its literal form. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eklepsen de Iakōb tēn kardian Labana* (ἔκλεψεν δὲ Ἰακὼβ τὴν καρδίαν Λαβάν) — "Jacob stole the heart of Laban" — an exact literal rendering of the Hebrew idiom.

---

**31:24 — "Neither good nor evil" (*mittov 'ad-ra'*, מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע):**
God's warning to Laban in the dream uses the Hebrew merism מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע — "from good to evil" — meaning: *say nothing at all to Jacob, neither good nor bad*. The prepositions מִן... עַד ("from... until/to") create a range that encompasses everything between the two poles — thus "neither good nor evil" = "nothing whatsoever." Laban repeats the same formula back to Jacob in v. 29: "the God of your father said to me... guard yourself from speaking... either good or evil." This merism is identical in form to the formula in Genesis 24:50 (Laban's own earlier speech!) and Numbers 24:13. EMET renders it "either good or evil" following the MT's merism structure. The LXX Vaticanus reads *apo tou kalou heōs tou ponērou* (ἀπὸ τοῦ καλοῦ ἕως τοῦ πονηροῦ) — "from the good to the evil" — a precise literal rendering of the merism.

---

**31:35 — "The way of women" (*derekh nashim*, דֶּרֶךְ נָשִׁים):**
Rachel's excuse — כִּי-דֶרֶךְ נָשִׁים לִי (*ki-derekh nashim li*, "for the way of women is upon me") — is the biblical idiom for menstruation. The word דֶּרֶךְ (*derekh*, "way, path, manner") used as a euphemism for the menstrual cycle is unique to this passage in the Hebrew Bible in this exact formulation. The claim serves a double purpose in the narrative: it is a plausible excuse for remaining seated without rising to greet her father (a significant social breach, since rising before a parent was obligatory), and it invokes a concept of ritual impurity that would make Laban reluctant to touch anything she was sitting on (cf. Leviticus 15:20: "Everything on which she lies during her impurity shall be unclean; everything also on which she sits shall be unclean"). Whether Rachel's claim is truthful or a deliberate deception is left ambiguous by the narrator. The dramatic irony of the teraphim hidden beneath her — the very cult objects her father seeks — rendered inaccessible by the invocation of female impurity, is among the most pointed moments of satirical narrative in the entire Jacob cycle. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ta kata tas gynaikas* (τὰ κατὰ τὰς γυναῖκας) — "the things according to [the custom of] women" — a circumspect paraphrase. EMET renders the MT literally: "the way of women."

---

**31:38–40 — Jacob's Pastoral Defense — "The torn animal" and "fled sleep":**
Jacob's speech in vv. 38–40 is a rhetorical masterpiece of pastoral accountability. Three specific obligations of a shepherd in Israelite law (cf. Exodus 22:10–13) are invoked:

1. **Miscarried/aborted animals** (*lo' shikkelu*, לֹא שִׁכֵּלוּ, v. 38): The verb *shikkel* (שִׁכֵּל, Pi'el of *shākal*, "to bereave, to cause miscarriage") means the ewes and nanny-goats did not miscarry or lose young under his care — a mark of excellent husbandry.

2. **The torn animal** (*tĕrephah*, טְרֵפָה, v. 39): Normally, if a wild animal tore livestock, the shepherd could bring the remains as evidence and be absolved of liability (Exodus 22:12). Jacob says he bore the loss himself — "I would bear the loss of it; from my hand you required it" (*'anokhi 'ăkhattennah miyyādi tĕvaqshennah*). The verb חָטָא (*khātā'*) in Piel (*'ăkhattennah*) here means "to pay a penalty, to compensate, to make good" — not the more common "to sin." EMET renders it "I bore the loss of it" to capture the legal compensation sense.

3. **Day-heat and night-frost** (v. 40): Jacob's vivid description — חֹרֶב (*khorev*, "scorching heat, drought") by day and קֶרַח (*qerakÌ†*, "frost, ice") by night — captures the extreme temperature swings of the Near Eastern highland pastures. The poetic verb וַתִּדַּד שְׁנָתִי מֵעֵינָי (*wattiddad shĕnati me'eynay*, "and my sleep fled from my eyes") uses the rare verb דָּדַד (*dādad*, "to wander, to flee") for sleeplessness — sleep as something that runs away. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai apōcheto ho hypnos apo tōn ophthalmōn mou* (καὶ ἀπῴχετο ὁ ὕπνος ἀπὸ τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν μου) — "and sleep departed from my eyes" — a fine rendering of the idiom.

---

**31:42 — The Fear of Isaac (*Pachad Yitschaq*, פַּחַד יִצְחָק) — Divine Epithet:**
The epithet פַּחַד יִצְחָק (*Pakhad Yitschaq*) appears here (v. 42) and again in v. 53. It is one of the most unusual divine epithets in the entire Hebrew Bible, occurring only in these two verses. Two principal interpretations exist:

1. **"The Fear of Isaac"** (traditional rendering): From the root פַּחַד (*pakhad*, "to fear, to tremble") — "the Terror / the Dread of Isaac" — the deity before whom Isaac trembles, or who inspires fear. This would be a striking epithet emphasizing God's overwhelming and terrifying holiness, comparable to the "Fear of the LORD" (*yir'at YHWH*) elsewhere.

2. **"The Kinsman/Relative of Isaac"** (Albright, Cross, and others): Some scholars have proposed that *pakhad* here derives from a different root meaning "thigh, loins" (cognate to Arabic *fakhidh*) and means "Kinsman" or "Clan-protector of Isaac" — a title reflecting the ancient Near Eastern concept of a patron deity as the divine ancestor or kinsman-protector of a family. This reading would parallel epithets like "the Shield of Abraham" (Gen 15:1) and "the Mighty One of Jacob" (Gen 49:24).

Both readings are philologically possible. The first is the more natural reading of the extant Hebrew word *pakhad* and is supported by the immediate context (v. 42 pairs "the Fear of Isaac" with God who *sees Jacob's affliction* and *rebukes* Laban — emphases on terror and judgment). The second is a scholarly inference from comparative Semitics. EMET renders it "the Fear of Isaac" following the MT vocalization and the straightforward sense of the word *pakhad*, but notes the alternative interpretation. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ho phobos Isaak* (ὁ φόβος Ἰσαάκ) — "the Fear of Isaac" — confirming the traditional rendering and the LXX translators' reading of *pakhad* as "fear."

---

**31:47 — Jegar-sahadutha (Aramaic) and Gal-ed (Hebrew) — Bilingual Naming:**
This verse is one of only a few places in the Hebrew Bible where Aramaic and Hebrew stand side by side as distinct languages with explicit identification. Laban, as an Aramean, names the heap in Aramaic: יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא (*Yĕgar Sāhĕdûthā'*) — literally "heap of witness" in Aramaic (*yĕgar* = heap, *sāhĕd* = witness, *ûthā'* = Aramaic suffix). Jacob, as a Hebrew, names it גַּלְעֵד (*Gal'ed*) — "heap of witness" in Hebrew (*gal* = heap, *'ed* = witness). Both names carry identical meaning in different languages, and their juxtaposition dramatizes the cultural and linguistic boundary being established: this is not merely a geographic marker but a linguistic frontier between Aram and Israel, between Laban's world and Jacob's. The narrative proceeds to use *Gal'ed* (Gilead) as the name that endures — the Hebrew name, not the Aramaic, becomes the name of the territory. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Bōmos tou marturos* (Βωμὸς τοῦ μάρτυρος) for the Greek — "Altar of the witness" — but this conflates the heap (*gal*) with an altar (*bōmos*), which is not the Hebrew. The LXX may be reading *gal* through the lens of the altar-building tradition. EMET transliterates both names: Jegar-sahadutha (Aramaic) and Gal-ed (Hebrew), and notes their identical meaning.

---

**31:49 — Mizpah (*hammitspah*, הַמִּצְפָּה) — Watchpost:**
The word הַמִּצְפָּה (*hammitspah*, "the Mizpah / the Watchtower / the Watchpost") derives from the root צָפָה (*tsāphāh*, "to watch, to look out, to keep watch"). The naming explanation in v. 49 is explicit: the LORD shall *watch* (יִצֶף *yitseph*, from the same root) between them. Mizpah thus means "watchpost" — the place where God keeps watch over the covenant between Jacob and Laban when they are out of each other's sight. Several sites in the land of Canaan later bear the name Mizpah (Judg 10:17; 11:11; 1 Sam 7:5–12; etc.), all with the sense of "watchtower/lookout." The Mizpah benediction — "May the LORD watch between me and you when we are hidden from each other" — is often quoted in modern usage as a blessing, but in its original context it functions as a *warning*: God watches *because neither party trusts the other* when they cannot see each other. It is a covenant-threat as much as a blessing. EMET transliterates "Mizpah" and notes the root. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Skopia* (Σκοπιά) — "the Watchtower/Lookout" — translating the Hebrew name rather than transliterating it, and uses *ephorā* (ἐφορᾷ) — "watches over" — for *yitseph*, preserving the etymological wordplay.

---

**31:53 — "The God of Abraham and the God of Nahor... shall judge" — Plural Verb:**
The verb יִשְׁפְּטוּ (*yishpĕtu*) in v. 53 is a **plural** form — "they shall judge" — despite the subject being "the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father." The plural verb is theologically striking: it uses the plural as if "the God of Abraham" and "the God of Nahor" are invoked as distinct divine parties, or as if the plural construction treats the two divine epithets as a compound subject requiring a plural verb. Some ancient interpreters took this as evidence that Laban is swearing by different gods (the god of Abraham separately from the god of Nahor), while Jacob swears only by the single "Fear of Isaac" (v. 53b). The Samaritan Pentateuch and some manuscripts read the singular יִשְׁפֹּט (*yishpot*) — "he shall judge" — harmonizing the grammar with monotheistic expectation. The MT plural is the lectio difficilior and is retained here. EMET renders it "shall judge" (plural force) and notes the variant. The LXX Vaticanus reads *krinai* (κρῖναι) — an aorist infinitive — rather than a finite plural verb, which sidesteps the grammatical issue entirely.

---

**31:54 — "Ate bread" (*wayyokhlu lekhem*, וַיֹּאכְלוּ לֶחֶם) — Covenant Meal:**
The covenant meal on the mountain — Jacob offering a sacrifice (*zevakh*) and then calling his kinsmen to eat bread — is a formal covenantal ratification act. "Eating bread" in this context is not casual dining but the sealing of a covenant: sharing a meal in the presence of God (who received the sacrifice) ratified the agreement in the most solemn form available. Compare Genesis 26:30 (Isaac and Abimelech's covenant meal), Exodus 24:11 (the elders of Israel eating and drinking before God at Sinai). The sacrifice (*zevakh*, זֶבַח) here is not specified as a burnt offering (*'olah*) but as a "slaughter-sacrifice" — a fellowship or peace offering shared between human parties (cf. Leviticus 3). The LXX Vaticanus reads *ethysen thysian* (ἔθυσεν θυσίαν) — "sacrificed a sacrifice" — exact rendering of the cognate accusative *wayyizbakh zevakh*.

---

**31:55 — Verse Numbering Note — MT vs. LXX:**
In the Masoretic Text (and thus in most English Bibles), v. 55 is the final verse of Genesis 31. In the LXX and some English versification traditions, this verse appears as Genesis 32:1. The content — Laban's early morning farewell, his kissing his sons and daughters and blessing them, and his departure — is identical; only the chapter-verse assignment differs. EMET follows the MT versification (55 verses), and this translation numbers v. 55 accordingly. The LXX Vaticanus, on Vaticanus page 29, shows the new *CAP. XXXVI* marker beginning with what the LXX calls 32:1 — confirming the versification difference between the traditions.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 27–29):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 27–29 carry Genesis 31 across three pages in three-column uncial. The chapter margin notations visible are *CAP. XXXIII* (page 27), *CAP. XXXIIII* (page 28), and *CAP. XXXV* / *CAP. XXXVI* (page 29), reflecting the ancient Greek chapter division system which does not align with the MT chapter divisions.

Key LXX readings for Genesis 31:

1. **31:1** — LXX *tēn doxan tautēn* — "this glory" — for MT *kol-hakkavod hazzeh* — exact conceptual equivalence.

2. **31:7** — LXX *deka amnas* — "ten lambs" — for MT *'aseret monim* — "ten times" — a striking divergence. The LXX may have read *monim* as a measure (like *maneh*) and interpreted the measure as a quantity of lambs.

3. **31:10** — LXX adds *en tō kairō ote ekonon ta probata* — "in the season when the flocks conceived" — as a clarifying expansion; MT is more laconic.

4. **31:13** — LXX *ho Theos ho ophtheis soi en topō Theou* — "the God who appeared to you in the place of God" — for *ha-El Bet-El* — rendering "Bethel" interpretively as "the place of God."

5. **31:19** — LXX *ta eidōla* — "the idols" — for MT *hattĕraphim* — a theologically loaded translation that interprets rather than transliterates.

6. **31:20** — LXX *eklepsen de Iakōb tēn kardian Labana* — exact rendering of the "stole the heart" idiom.

7. **31:24** — LXX *apo tou kalou heōs tou ponērou* — "from the good to the evil" — exact rendering of the merism.

8. **31:42** — LXX *ho phobos Isaak* — "the Fear of Isaac" — confirming the standard rendering of *Pachad Yitschaq*.

9. **31:47** — LXX renders Laban's Aramaic *Yĕgar Sāhĕdûthā'* interpretively as *Bōmos tou marturos* ("Altar of the witness") and Jacob's *Gal-ed* as *Bōmos tou marturos* similarly — flattening the bilingual contrast present in the MT. The distinctive Aramaic/Hebrew bilingualism of the MT is not preserved in the LXX.

10. **31:49** — LXX *Skopia* — "Watchtower" — translating rather than transliterating *Mitspah*, and *ephorā ho Theos* — "God watches/oversees" — for *yitseph YHWH*, preserving the etymological wordplay.

11. **31:53** — LXX uses the aorist infinitive *krinai* rather than a plural finite verb, thereby avoiding the grammatically plural *yishpĕtu* of the MT.

12. **31:55 / 32:1** — The LXX begins a new chapter (CAP. XXXVI) with what the MT numbers 31:55, visible on Vaticanus page 29. This versification difference should be noted in all cross-reference work.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 31 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary Hebrew witness) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary Greek witness) are the principal manuscript sources examined for this chapter.
